blocks.text: I’ll separate the options into: truly free local voices, browser-built-in voices, and cloud APIs with free tiers. For your LingoWatch use case, the main issue is natural voice quality versus cost and latency. For LingoWatch, the best free TTS setup is:

sections.plainText: I’ll separate the options into: truly free local voices, browser-built-in voices, and cloud APIs with free tiers. For your LingoWatch use case, the main issue is natural voice quality versus cost and latency. For LingoWatch, the best free TTS setup is:

sections.title: Best choice for you

blocks.text: Best choice for you
Use Web Speech API first + add local Kokoro/Piper later.
1. Web Speech API — easiest, 100% free
This runs inside the user’s browser. No API key, no server, no Google billing.

items: “Read sentence”
“Repeat subtitle”
“Read selected word”
Language-learning UI like Language Reactor
Voice quality depends on the user’s device/browser
You cannot reliably save audio as MP3
Voices are different on Mac, Windows, iPhone, Android

blocks.text: MDN says Web Speech API includes SpeechSynthesis, which is browser text-to-speech. Example for your frontend:

sections.plainText:
Best choice for you

Use Web Speech API first + add local Kokoro/Piper later.

1. Web Speech API — easiest, 100% free

This runs inside the user’s browser. No API key, no server, no Google billing.

“Read sentence”
“Repeat subtitle”
“Read selected word”
Language-learning UI like Language Reactor
Voice quality depends on the user’s device/browser
You cannot reliably save audio as MP3
Voices are different on Mac, Windows, iPhone, Android

MDN says Web Speech API includes SpeechSynthesis, which is browser text-to-speech. Example for your frontend:

sections.title: Best free local AI voices

blocks.text: Best free local AI voices
2. Kokoro TTS — best free local quality

Kokoro is open-weight, small, fast, and Apache-2.0 licensed, so it is better for a real project than many random TTS models. It has an 82M parameter model and can be deployed locally or on your own server.

items: More natural voices
Backend-generated audio
Saving.wav files
Production project

blocks.text: 3. Piper TTS — fastest local offline option
Piper is a fast local neural TTS system. The newer Piper repo says it can be installed with pip install piper-tts and includes command-line, web server, and Python API options.

items: Very fast local TTS
Low server cost
Offline usage
Many voices
Quality is usually less natural than Kokoro/ElevenLabs
Newer Piper repo is GPL-3.0, so check license carefully before commercial release.

sections.plainText:
Best free local AI voices

2. Kokoro TTS — best free local quality

Kokoro is open-weight, small, fast, and Apache-2.0 licensed, so it is better for a real project than many random TTS models. It has an 82M parameter model and can be deployed locally or on your own server.

More natural voices
Backend-generated audio
Saving.wav files
Production project

3. Piper TTS — fastest local offline option

Piper is a fast local neural TTS system. The newer Piper repo says it can be installed with pip install piper-tts and includes command-line, web server, and Python API options.

Very fast local TTS
Low server cost
Offline usage
Many voices
Quality is usually less natural than Kokoro/ElevenLabs
Newer Piper repo is GPL-3.0, so check license carefully before commercial release.

sections.title: Free cloud API options

blocks.text: Free cloud API options
4. Azure Speech — good free tier
Azure Speech gives 0.5 million neural TTS characters free per month on the Free F0 tier.

items: Stable voices
Easier than hosting your own model

blocks.text: 5. Google Cloud Text-to-Speech — usable with your Google credit

Google Cloud TTS requires billing to be enabled and charges by character, but free monthly character allowances may apply. Google says the $300 trial credit can be used for covered Google Cloud products, but after upgrading to paid billing, you can be charged for usage beyond free tier/credit. For your case: you can use your Google Cloud credit for Google Cloud Text-to-Speech, but watch billing limits carefully.
6. ElevenLabs — best quality, but free is tiny
ElevenLabs has a free plan with 10k credits/month, but the Starter plan is where they list a commercial license. Good for testing only. Not best for your full LingoWatch app unless you pay.

sections.plainText:
Free cloud API options

4. Azure Speech — good free tier

Azure Speech gives 0.5 million neural TTS characters free per month on the Free F0 tier.

Stable voices
Easier than hosting your own model

5. Google Cloud Text-to-Speech — usable with your Google credit

Google Cloud TTS requires billing to be enabled and charges by character, but free monthly character allowances may apply. Google says the $300 trial credit can be used for covered Google Cloud products, but after upgrading to paid billing, you can be charged for usage beyond free tier/credit. For your case: you can use your Google Cloud credit for Google Cloud Text-to-Speech, but watch billing limits carefully.

6. ElevenLabs — best quality, but free is tiny

ElevenLabs has a free plan with 10k credits/month, but the Starter plan is where they list a commercial license. Good for testing only. Not best for your full LingoWatch app unless you pay.

sections.title: My recommendation for your project

blocks.text: My recommendation for your project

items: Frontend Web Speech API for free instant TTS.
Add Kokoro local backend for better AI voice.
Cache generated audio so the same sentence is not generated again.
Use Azure free tier or Google Cloud TTS only when you need higher-quality stable cloud voices.

blocks.text: Your best architecture: Copy this prompt to your coding AI:

I want to add free TTS to my LingoWatch project.Implement two TTS modes:1. Browser TTS mode using the Web Speech API:- Add a reusable speakText(text, lang, rate) function.- It should work for subtitle sentences, selected words, and repeat buttons.- Add play, stop, and replay controls.- Use SpeechSynthesisUtterance.- Auto-detect language when possible, otherwise allow lang like en-US, tr-TR, ar-SA.2. Local AI TTS backend mode:- Create an API route /api/tts.- Input: text, lang, voice, speed.- Generate audio using local Kokoro TTS or Piper TTS.- Cache audio by hashing text + voice + speed.- If cached audio exists, return it immediately.- Save generated audio in a public or storage folder.- Return JSON: { audioUrl, cached: true/false }.Default behavior:- Use Web Speech API first because it is free.- Allow user to switch to AI voice mode.- Add clean error handling when TTS is not supported.- Make it suitable for a language-learning app like Language Reactor.
For now, start with Web Speech API. It costs $0, works immediately, and is enough for your first LingoWatch version.

sections.plainText:
My recommendation for your project

Frontend Web Speech API for free instant TTS.
Add Kokoro local backend for better AI voice.
Cache generated audio so the same sentence is not generated again.
Use Azure free tier or Google Cloud TTS only when you need higher-quality stable cloud voices.

Your best architecture: Copy this prompt to your coding AI:

I want to add free TTS to my LingoWatch project.Implement two TTS modes:1. Browser TTS mode using the Web Speech API:- Add a reusable speakText(text, lang, rate) function.- It should work for subtitle sentences, selected words, and repeat buttons.- Add play, stop, and replay controls.- Use SpeechSynthesisUtterance.- Auto-detect language when possible, otherwise allow lang like en-US, tr-TR, ar-SA.2. Local AI TTS backend mode:- Create an API route /api/tts.- Input: text, lang, voice, speed.- Generate audio using local Kokoro TTS or Piper TTS.- Cache audio by hashing text + voice + speed.- If cached audio exists, return it immediately.- Save generated audio in a public or storage folder.- Return JSON: { audioUrl, cached: true/false }.Default behavior:- Use Web Speech API first because it is free.- Allow user to switch to AI voice mode.- Add clean error handling when TTS is not supported.- Make it suitable for a language-learning app like Language Reactor.

For now, start with Web Speech API. It costs $0, works immediately, and is enough for your first LingoWatch version.

I’ll separate the options into: truly free local voices, browser-built-in voices, and cloud APIs with free tiers. For your LingoWatch use case, the main issue is natural voice quality versus cost and latency. For LingoWatch, the best free TTS setup is:

Best choice for you

Use Web Speech API first + add local Kokoro/Piper later.

1. Web Speech API — easiest, 100% free

This runs inside the user’s browser. No API key, no server, no Google billing.

“Read sentence”
“Repeat subtitle”
“Read selected word”
Language-learning UI like Language Reactor
Voice quality depends on the user’s device/browser
You cannot reliably save audio as MP3
Voices are different on Mac, Windows, iPhone, Android

MDN says Web Speech API includes SpeechSynthesis, which is browser text-to-speech. Example for your frontend:

Newer Piper repo is GPL-3.0, so check license carefully before commercial release.

My recommendation for your project

I want to add free TTS to my LingoWatch project.Implement two TTS modes:1. texts.title: What to Know About the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire
texts.author: https://www.nytimes.com/by/amelia-nierenberg

sections.title: What to Know About the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire

blocks.text: The cease-fire agreement between Iran and the United States has been greeted with relief around the world. But what comes next is far from certain. Both sides claimed victory after the announcement of the deal, which was brokered by Pakistan. Iran says it will allow ships safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial corridor for the world’s oil and gas, if they coordinate with its military. President Trump had threatened to destroy Iran’s “whole civilization” unless it reopened the strait. But shipping companies seem hesitant to return to the waterway, at least immediately. Scattered attacks in the Gulf continued early on Wednesday, hours after the agreement was reached. Israel made clear that it would continue its military campaign against Hezbollah in Lebanon. Subscribe to The Times to read as many articles as you like. is a Times reporter covering international news from London.

texts.title: Video File Player Upgrades - News from the Team - Language Learning with Netflix Forum

sections.title: Video File Player Upgrades - News from the Team - Language Learning with Netflix Forum

blocks.text: You have selected 0 posts. Hello users. I made some upgrades to the video file player. You can try it here: Language Reactor

items: Video files with AC3/EAC3 audio can be played now (the audio is transcoded).
You can now select non-default audio tracks.
You can make use of subtitles that are embedded in the video file (SRT and ASS formats).
You can run ASR on the audio track to get subtitles.

blocks.text: Regarding formats, most.mkv and.mp4 video files should work now, I’d guess 95%. AVI videos will not work, but they are more rare. If it’s not working for you on a video, you can PM me with the torrent magnet link, I can try get it working.
Hello! Looks like you’re enjoying the discussion, but you haven’t signed up for an account yet.

Tired of scrolling through the same posts? When you create an account you’ll always come back to where you left off. With an account you can also be notified of new replies, save bookmarks, and use likes to thank others. We can all work together to make this community great.

sections.plainText:
You have selected 0 posts. Hello users. I made some upgrades to the video file player. You can try it here: Language Reactor

Video files with AC3/EAC3 audio can be played now (the audio is transcoded).
You can now select non-default audio tracks.
You can make use of subtitles that are embedded in the video file (SRT and ASS formats).
You can run ASR on the audio track to get subtitles.

Regarding formats, most.mkv and.mp4 video files should work now, I’d guess 95%. AVI videos will not work, but they are more rare. If it’s not working for you on a video, you can PM me with the torrent magnet link, I can try get it working.

Hello! Looks like you’re enjoying the discussion, but you haven’t signed up for an account yet.

Tired of scrolling through the same posts? When you create an account you’ll always come back to where you left off. With an account you can also be notified of new replies, save bookmarks, and use likes to thank others. We can all work together to make this community great.
